For an industrial robot built for the rigors of factories and power plants, tidying up a living room may seem like a light day at the office for Spot. Yet, this demonstration represents the promise of AI models in robotics. In this case, Google DeepMind visual-language model (VLM) Gemini Robotics-ER 1.5 was empowering Spot with embodied reasoning.

So we’ve officially reached the point where you can give a robot a to-do list… and it might even walk your dog better than you do.
Jokes aside – this is a huge leap. Moving from coding every step to simply telling a robot what to do changes the game entirely.
The real breakthrough isn’t the demo – it’s the shift toward robots that understand context, not just commands.
Question is: are we ready for machines that don’t just execute tasks, but actually decide how to do them?

Jagan Nivas P
3w
The tool-calling architecture is clean — Gemini Robotics acting as the operator, tools as the API bridge, Spot handling the physical execution. Real leverage for developers who already work with the SDK.
One thing the blog makes explicit: "until the robot powers on, Gemini Robotics has no context for what tasks we might ask it to perform." That's the right scope for this layer — but it also points to what sits above it in a real home deployment. Persistent identity (who is giving the instruction — homeowner, guest, child), memory across sessions, social awareness when a second person enters the room, privacy constraints on what the robot retains after it's done tidying.
None of that falls out of the VLM or the motion stack. It's a separate layer that has to be designed independently and composed cleanly with what Boston Dynamics and DeepMind have built here. Been working on exactly that — cognitive runtime for physical AI, local deployment, persistent memory with privacy enforced at the database layer. The task reasoning breakthrough makes the social-cognitive layer more urgent, not less.

🚨 Boston Dynamics just dropped their first virtual AI agent: Bobbi 🚨
Bobbi is an AI assistant designed to help users explore their robotics solutions, identify the right tools for specific applications, and streamline operational improvement.
While we’ve come to associate Boston Dynamics with high-powered robots leaping, flipping, and hauling cargo - Bobbi takes a different path:
💡 A virtual AI agent focused on language, reasoning, and memory but still built with the same world-class robotic brainpower.
Bobbi represents the next wave of intelligent, accessible automation:
 ✅ Scalable
 ✅ User-driven
 ✅ Available 24/7 to support ops, logistics, and engineering teams
In other words, it’s not just the robots getting smarter. The way we buy, deploy, and scale robots is evolving too.
🧠 What does this signal for the future of how we interact with robots and automation?
Could AI agents like Bobbi:
Transform how engineering teams evaluate and implement robotic solutions?
Redefine product discovery across complex, technical industries?
Become the new standard for customer-facing automation in mobility and manufacturing?
👇 We're curious - how would you see a tool like this fitting into your world?
📸 Image credit: Boston Dynamics
#BostonDynamics #Bobbi #AI #Robotics #MobilityInnovation
